chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Senior Capstone Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Development of system integration skills
for solving real-world problems in computer science; significant team software
project that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer science
specializations; projects require design, implementation, documentation and
demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and
teamwork. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE
331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or
CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing and other
relevant disciplines to identify solutions.
• Design, implement and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given set of
computing requirements in the context of the program's discipline.
• Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.
• Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgements in computing
practice based on legal and ethical principles.
• Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate
to the program's discipline.
• Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals to produce
computing-based solutions.

chunks.title: To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component, with the C course communication requirement below:
chunks.content:
To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component, with the C course
communication requirement below:
• A minimum of 35% of your final grade is based on written and oral communication
assignments.
• Of this communication component, at least 70% is based on individually written work
and individual public speaking; group work does not count toward this standard.
• To receive C credit, students must pass both the written and public speaking
components of the course. Failure to pass either component will result in failing the
course, regardless of performance on other assignments.
• Each student will produce at least 1250 words of individually written work and
deliver a minimum of 5 minutes of individual public speaking.
• Assignments fulfilling this requirement include individual progress reports, project
proposals, final technical documentation, and individual presentations or demonstrations.
• Resumes and related materials are accepted only if specific to the major; take-home essay
exams may count if they allow drafting and revision. Oral presentation slides, scripts, web
sites, and posters count when accompanied by individual presentations.
No more than 10% of the communication grade may be assessed by undergraduate aides,
and all grading by aides is supervised by the instructor.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
is course is governed by ABET (Accreditation Board for Engineering and
Technology), which provides assurance that a college or university program meets
the quality standards of the profession, by doing the following:
• Verifies that your educational experience meets the global standard for
technical education in your profession.
• Enhances your employment opportunities—multinational corporations
require graduation from an accredited program.
• Supports your entry to a technical profession through licensure,
registration, and certification—all of which often require graduation from an
ABET-accredited program as a minimum qualification.
• Establishes your eligibility for federal student loans, grants, and/or
scholarships.
• Paves the way for you to work globally, because ABET accreditation is
recognized worldwide through international agreements, and other countries'
national accrediting systems are based on the ABET model.
To comply with ABET, students must demonstrate their abilities in outcomes above, which will be
evaluated by external industrial affiliate members during your final presentation.
The final grade you will receive in the class will be based on points accumulated during the
semester. Thus, both continued progress (the process) and the quality of your product (and other
deliverables) will determine your grade. Your grade is based on the performance of your team
and your individual contribution.
The grading scale is as follows:
F < 60% ≤ D < 70% < C < 80% ≤ B < 90% ≤A
We do not curve in this class, however, provide you with the opportunity to earn an A, hence an
89.9 is a B. We teach proficiency, which means that we have clear expectations in terms of what
you should complete and what knowledge you should have obtained by the end of the course to
succeed and to earn an A.
The grade distribution is as follows:
1. Professional Responsibility 5%
2. Project Management 10%
3. Project Implementation 15%
4. Project Evaluation 20%
5. Project Documentation 20%
6. Project Presentation 20%
7. Quizzes 10%
TOTAL 100%
• You will not receive a passing grade in the class if you do not have a tested working system
by the due date. The lower-weight items will significantly help you succeed in your higher-
weighted items.
• Your peer feedback responses will be calculated with the instructors' assessment which
can either increase / decrease each member's overall semester score.
Instructors will base their assessment on one or more of the following: class attendance,
punctuality, participation during class, timely submission of assignments/deliverables, peer
feedback results, other informal feedback from teammates or TAs, and other actions which
positively/negatively contribute to the success of the team.
• Any team member who does not participate, after interventions have been attempted, will
automatically get at least 1 letter grade below the lowest grade of any of the team
members
• To complete the course and receive a grade for the class, students are required to do the
following:
• All final deliverables must be submitted and approved by the teaching team, borrowed
items (e.g., keys, books, equipment) returned to their proper location or to their owner, and
work areas cleaned. All team members are required to be present at the time of the final
delivery.
• Participate in either a project presentation or an exit interview with industry practitioners as
part of the ABET review. If the course section is selected to be reviewed as part of the
capstone project review the section's students are excused from participating in the exit
interviews.
• Complete the assigned ethics related assignments.
• Written and oral communication assignments constitute at least 35% of the total course
grade, with no less than 70% of this graded individually. Students must successfully
complete and pass both written and oral assignments to pass the course. These
components will be explicitly identified in the assignment schedule and rubric.
1. Professional Responsibility (5%)
This category consists of feedback on peers' performance in the project and ethics knowledge,
specifically completion of the assigned ethics assignment.
You will give feedback on the performance and contribution of your other team members several
times throughout the semester. Prompt submission of peer feedback will be the basis of this
grade. NOTE: The instructor team has the right to withhold credit for submission of peer
feedback, due to late submission, 3 days after the due date, unless you have prior approval for
late submission.
Failure to submit your peer feedback for all your fellow team members after two reminders will
result in an automatic 5% deduction of the overall grade. This policy is in place to ensure timely
distribution of valuable peer feedback and cannot be reversed once the grades are posted in
Canvas.
Regardless of whether you get credit or not, your final grade cannot be finalized until you
complete all your peer feedback.
2. Project Management (10%)
Artifacts
These represent submissions that are required during project initiation, such as skills survey,
resume, and other items required in Canvas.
Group Updates, Individual Updates
Each team submits a weekly report as a group and individually, using the given templates. The
group reports communicate the team's accomplishments during the previous period, which need
to be measurable, and planned accomplishments for the following week.
Reports
These represent project management related reports.
Other deliverables
You are expected to choose a software engineering process model and create the corresponding
artifacts. For example, if the team chooses to use Agile Scrum, then expected artifacts are sprint
& product backlogs, user stories, etc.
Depending on the project, project artifacts to be submitted include (but not limited to):
a. Data: Test data, initial seed data, etc.
b. Designs: schematics, data sheets, etc.
c. Tools: freeware software tools, compilers, etc.
d. Documentation: project related documents you are asked to submit
e. Evaluation: evaluation related documents you are asked to submit
f. Audiovisual media: close-up pictures of your system, raw footage, etc.
g. Evidence of live presentations given (e.g., program, acknowledgement letters,
etc.)
h. Spare parts and supplies
i. Installed final application on one or more devices
j. Invoices / Receipts / Forms: See next section on Purchases & Reimbursements
Purchases and Reimbursements
• Accurate Forms: Please ensure that purchase order (PO) forms submitted to
the department are accurate and complete (e.g., specifications). Submitting an
incomplete PO will result in the team being prevented from submitting additional
POs; all additional purchases will have to be made by team members who will be
reimbursed.
• In-Stock Items: Please verify that items are in stock before submitting a PO and
provide alternative vendors if an item is likely to go out of stock (e.g., if the vendor
only has a few units left). If you suspect that a critical item may become
backordered, it may be better if you purchase it directly since POs may take
several days to go through.
• Reimbursements Deadline: Reimbursement requests are due at the time of
final deliverables. To minimize overhead, we will only reimburse up to five
purchase orders (of your choice); this policy also encourages teams to plan their
purchases in advance. Overnight or express delivery will not be reimbursed
unless approved in advance by the instructor. Reimbursement of sales taxes is
not allowed by the university; please make sure to bring tax-exempt forms with
you whenever you make local purchases (or purchases within the state).
• Equipment Return: The equipment and materials (e.g., keys, books, equipment)
that you purchased or borrowed do not belong to you, they belong to Texas
A&M University, hence they need to be returned to their proper location, to
their owner, or to the instructor team.
Lastly, we expect that the facilities used by the project team have been restored to an acceptable
condition as confirmed by the instructor team.
NOTE: Grades will not be assigned until all project deliverables have been turned in or
completed. All team members are required to participate in making sure deliverables are
completed and delivered.
3. Project Implementation (15%)
Code (with corresponding documentation) will be submitted in the class GitHub repository,
Version Control
These represent the set-up of your version control system and regular commits to your
repository.
Code/Result & Documentation
Documentation (e.g., GitHub Readme) and code will be critiqued on its compliance with
requirements, correctness in proper execution, and maintainability as indicated by the effort
needed to understand the details of your system, by people external to the team.
4. Project Evaluation (20%)
Internal Testing
You are expected to assess that the expected deliverables of the project meet implicit and explicit
(e.g., functionality) requirements. Expectations include developing automated test suites and
multi-phase testing (unit / integration / system testing). The definition of success is related to how
well you prepared and implemented your evaluation plan to meet your team's quality goals (e.g.,
test coverage). You are expected to submit your test results to get credit.
User Acceptance Testing Results: at least twenty participants (see the instructor if you have
circumstances that warrant having less than twenty)
This evaluates how well you prepared and executed the user acceptance test, including
submission of the user acceptance test results. Note that your grade cannot be finalized unless
completion of user acceptance test.
** If you intend to publish your results, you will need to complete an IRB (ask instructor for
guidance)
5. Project Documentation (20%)
To fulfill the communication requirement of the class, each team is required to submit
documentation with some of the key documents described below.
Individual Proposal (for student proposed projects)
Each student will look for a problem or need of a real customer or beneficiary and develop a
problem proposal for discussion with the bigger group. Thereafter, a set of viable problems will be
presented to the class as candidates for capstone projects. Reviews from your classmates on
your proposals will help define the final project idea. Likewise, you will review your classmates'
work to help refine their ideas and potentially find teammates with similar interests. Note:
Identification of a 'real' beneficiary is required.
Problem Exploration and Discussion
Scenario 1 – projects with sponsors: You will describe your approach to the given problem
statement, get your work reviewed by your peers, and optionally give a review on your
classmates' work. When the team is formed, all proposed approaches are synthesized and
become part of the final report.
Scenario 2 – projects with no sponsors, just an external beneficiary: You will iterate through
potential project ideas which have a proven need / existing beneficiary and explore your chosen
project idea further.
Final Report
An overview of content in your final report is covered below. The final report will be written
incrementally throughout the semester with submission of two intermediate increments, prior to
submission of the final version. Your final report will be evaluated on its thoroughness and
effectiveness in communicating key aspects of your system. Part of the grade is your review of
your peers' deliverables.
Abstract & Introduction
This is a summary of your final report that describes: your problem (with attention
grabber), how you plan to solve it, and expected outcome.
The introduction provides a brief overview of the project and helps the reader put into
context the design and implementation.
References / Related Work
To ensure that you have a strong understanding of existing relevant work you will perform
a literature review of related work to your team project. Each team member is expected to
identify a minimum number of relevant papers (details to be announced during class) for
their project to be referenced in their final report. These papers will be different for each
team member (e.g., a team of four with 5 references each will have twenty papers to
reference in their final report). Related work refers to each person reading two papers and
writing a reading summary on those two papers.
Engineering Standard
Engineering standards are factors that are related to your project and may bound or
constrain your project. Ethical concerns are included here.
Requirements
The requirements part of your methodology should clearly describe what your solution
seeks to achieve and what the user experience will be. You should describe your
minimum viable product, and any additional features required.
You may express requirements as narrative, feature lists, user stories, usage scenarios,
models, prototypes, etc.
User stories provide a high-level description of one or more features from the perspective
of an end user and make up your product backlog. Usage scenarios is the equivalent of
user stories in a non-agile process model.
Prototyping allows you to describe mockups you developed to demonstrate what your
solution will look like.
Design
The design part of your methodology should clearly describe how you will implement your
proposed solution, what the user experience will be, what tools you will use, and the risks
you anticipate.
Evaluation
The evaluation section describes procedures that you will use to assess that your system
does what it was designed to do.
Implementation, Results, Discussion
The implementation part of your methodology describes your system in detail including
individual components, tools used, your system hierarchy, and any other details for the
audience to understand how your system works.
The results section will summarize the outcome of your project and your evaluation to
show whether you achieved your definition of success.
The discussion section includes a reflection on the results.
Future Work, Conclusion, Annexes
Future work describes new opportunities and any lingering challenges that can be
undertaken in a future project.
The conclusion is a summary of your project with emphasis on the outcomes.
Annexes will contain the project plan, user manual, and additional information which
supports other sections of the final report, such as user stories which support the
requirements section, design models which support the design section, test cases which
support the evaluation section, and so forth.
6. Presentations (20%)
Checkpoints & Demo of System Progress,
External presentations (e.g., COE Project Showcase, CSCE Showcase)
Checkpoints refer to informal presentations on specific deliverables with the purpose of assessing
progress. Additionally, your team will present at a minimum of two other external venues, the
COE Project Showcase and CSCE Showcase. There may be other opportunities presented in
class which will provide you with extra practice in communicating your work and responding to
external reviews.
Posters, Presentation Slides & In-Class Presentation; Project Review-Industry
These opportunities allow you to communicate and demonstrate your deliverables (e.g.,
software) to your instructors, peers, and to industry panelists.
Demo Video & Appearance Release
The demo video is a 2-to-3-minute video which future generations of students can view as they
also embark on their capstone journey. Appearance release indicates your permission for us to
share the video and other artifacts which may have a picture / video of you.
Part of the grade is your reviews of your peers' deliverables.
7. Quizzes (10%)
The quizzes will evaluate students' review of any preparatory materials which help ensure that
students have reviewed them and are prepared for class.
Any item in this category are expected to be done in class, otherwise taking this outside of class
may constitute an honors violation, as determined by the honors council.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).
Course Specific Late Work Policy
Late work policy is defined as submission of work after the established deadline, not including
work submitted late due to an excused absence. Late work approvals must clearly link to an
excused absence request per Student Rule 7.
Late assignments cannot be accepted for credit, without a valid reason.
If you feel that you have a valid reason for submitting late work, even if it is not related to Student
Rule 7, submit a request to excuse the late submission (via a link to be provided) in advance of
the deadline (3 business days in advance is required, except in an emergency). We will carefully
review your request and will either approve or disapprove the request (with fairness and equity in
mind) if the request has been submitted in a timely manner.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Below is an overview of the overall schedule, subject to change. Refer to Canvas for the most up-to-date schedule.
| Week(s) | Topics / Activities |
|---|---|
| 1-4 | Introduction to Senior Capstone Design:
• Identify and formulate an engineering problem, how to function effectively in tean
your project fits in the context of other work, how to communicate the motivation,
and ethical considerations for the system, needs & requirements identification an
to define success in the project, project management.
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management & other deliverables |
| 5-7 | Project Design, Implementation, & Evaluation
• Deriving a design based on needs and requirements identification
• How to create an evaluation plan according to the team's definition of success.
• Skill development in preparation for implementation
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation,
implementation, evaluation (e.g., testing), & other deliverables |
| 8-11 | Project Implementation & Evaluation
• Communicate the user interface design and design choices
• Continue implementation & evaluation, including testing & reviews
• Conduct user acceptance test to evaluate the success of their system.
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation,
implementation, evaluation (e.g., testing), & other deliverables |
| 12-14 | Project Implementation, Evaluation, & Presentations
• Complete user acceptance test to evaluate the success of their system.
• Determine results and their implications.
• Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation,
implementation, evaluation (e.g., testing), in-class & external demonstrations, & c
deliverables |
| 15 | Finalize project implementation, presentations, and deliverable submission
• Capstone project review with industry (if selected) or exit interviews with industry
• Final Assessment: Iterative progress on project management, documentation,
implementation, evaluation (e.g., testing), in-class & external demonstrations, & c
deliverables |

chunks.title: Course Expectations
chunks.content:
Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and
agreed to in the first 2 weeks of class.
The course provides direct instruction in writing and public speaking through lectures,
workshops, peer review, assignment models, and opportunities for practice and revision.
Formative feedback will be given on both writing and speaking assignments prior to final
grading, and students are expected to revise and improve major submissions and presentations
in response to instructor and peer evaluations.

chunks.title: Course Copyright
chunks.content:
The materials used within this course are copyrighted and include, but are not limited to, the
syllabi, quizzes, exams, homework, online handouts, course videos, audio, and visual recordings
of classes, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or
distribute these materials, unless permission is explicitly granted.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Writing: Use of Al tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to
use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any Al-generated material that
informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your reference
list). Using an Al tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as academic
dishonesty.
See https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html fo
r information how to properly cite Al tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts
Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-
CTE GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf)
Computer Code. In principle you may submit Al-generated code, or code that is based on or
derived from Al-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments:
you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. Al tools may help you
avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your
responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.
Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for
improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The
submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe (2023)
“Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project http://sentientsyllabus.org..)

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Attendance is important. Not attending weekly class meetings harms the other members of
your group and makes it much more difficult for the instructor to assess your contributions to the
group effort. Due to the undue burden your frequent absences have on the rest of the team,
more than five unexcused absences are an automatic failure.
The instructors will evaluate your attendance through frequent quizzes, meetings, participation in
the discussions, participation in assignments, and/or contributions to the team. Upon instructors'
discretion, arriving to class late (> 5 minutes late), leaving early (> 5 minutes before class end),
and/or not participating (e.g., not taking a quiz, not being present for a status meeting, not
submitting in class work, and/or doing work not related to the class) can be considered an
unexcused absence, depending on the circumstances. Oversleeping, a term paper due, an exam
to cram for, etc., are not valid reasons. The instructor reserves the right to determine what
constitutes a “valid reason” on a case-by-case basis.
Absence / lateness during days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to
accomplish certain assignments (e.g., presenting your work outside of class, interviewing experts,
meeting with mentors, accessing technologies outside of the classroom, etc.) count toward the 5
unexcused absences, unless the absence / lateness are approved by the instructor in advance.
Students are expected to meet instructor's requirements for check in, activity reporting
requirements, and/or timely submission of-class assignments.
You are responsible for monitoring / tracking your unexcused absences.
Students experiencing personal injury or illness that is too severe for the student to attend class
qualify for an excused absence (see Student Rule 7, Section 7.2.2) assuming that documentation
and notification guidelines are followed.
